title: Representations are sheaves' for Legendrian 2-weaves
speaker: Kevin Sackel
abstract: Given a trivalent plane graph embedded in the Euclidean plane (up to isotopy), Treumann and Zaslow constructed and studied a certain associated Legendrian surface embedded in standard contact R5, nowadays referred to as a Legendrian 2-weave. Using gradient flow trees, Casals and Murphy computed its Legendrian contact dg-algebra with commutative coefficients (i.e. working over the group ring of the first homology group). We extend their computation to the non-commutative setting (i.e. working over the group ring of the fundamental group).

proof_stages.label: Representations-are-sheaves target and context
proof_stages.summary: The talk states the goal of verifying that rank-r representations of a Legendrian contact DGA agree with constructible sheaves, placing the comparison in the Nadler-Zaslow framework and among prior rank-one and higher-rank results.

proof_stages.label: Legendrian 2-weaves from trivalent plane graphs
proof_stages.summary: A trivalent plane graph G in S^2 is used to define an immersed branched double cover in S^2 x R with edge and vertex local models, whose Legendrian lift is the surface lambda_G in J^1(S^2).

proof_stages.label: Sheaf-side moduli as Grassmannian face labels
proof_stages.summary: The Treumann-Zaslow description identifies microlocal-rank-r constructible sheaves with assignments of r-planes in F^{2r} to faces of G, subject to transversality across adjacent faces.

proof_stages.label: Legendrian contact DGA computation framework
proof_stages.summary: The speaker sets up the Chekanov-Eliashberg DGA with noncommutative coefficients, recalls the Casals-Murphy gradient-flow-tree computation, and explains the generalization to pi_1 coefficients and functorial choices.

proof_stages.label: Fundamental group presentation from spanning-tree capping paths
proof_stages.summary: Choosing a tree spanning all but one vertex gives capping paths and generators lambda_e, lambda_bar_e for edge crossings, with vertex-loop words w_v giving a combinatorial presentation of pi_1(lambda_G).

proof_stages.label: Face Reeb chords and gradient-flow-tree differential
proof_stages.summary: Each face supplies a degree-one Reeb chord, and its differential is computed as a signed sum over adjacent vertices of the corresponding pi_1 words obtained from the gradient trajectories.

proof_stages.label: Representation equations
proof_stages.summary: A rank-r representation is an algebra map to r by r matrices sending pi_1 elements to invertibles and forcing every face differential to vanish, equivalently imposing explicit matrix-sum equations in the character variety.

proof_stages.label: Reconstruction of sheaf data and bijection
proof_stages.summary: From a representation one assigns GL_{2r} matrices across the regions cut by gradient trajectories and edges, then takes spans of the first r columns to obtain Grassmannian face labels; the representation equations make this well-defined and yield the desired bijection with the sheaf moduli.

examples.name: One-dimensional Legendrian knot from points on a circle
examples.note: The circle with three marked points is used as a lower-dimensional analogy that motivates replacing marked points on S^1 by a trivalent graph on S^2 to build a 2-weave.
examples.engagement_reason: he draws the S1-with-three-points model and explicitly constructs the immersed double cover/front projection and its Legendrian lift via the p-coordinate/contact condition

examples.name: Rank-one projective-line face coloring
examples.note: For r=1 the Grassmannian labeling becomes a coloring of graph faces by points of P^1 with adjacent faces distinct, making the sheaf moduli description concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: he specializes the sheaf/Grassmannian description to r=1, identifying the labels as P1 face colors and translating transversality into adjacent faces being distinct

examples.engagement_reason: he analyzes the one-dimensional satellite by closing the diagram, identifying the extra Reeb chords between top and bottom strands and explaining how trajectories can travel and break there
